LOADING…

📑 Atomic Orbital Theory — Complete Reference

§1 — Early Atomic Models & Classical Failures
Dalton's Atomic Theory (1808)
Matter is composed of indivisible atoms. All atoms of an element are identical. Atoms combine in fixed ratios to form compounds. Atoms cannot be created or destroyed in chemical reactions.
  • Postulates: (1) Matter is made of atoms. (2) Atoms of same element are identical. (3) Compounds = fixed atom ratios. (4) No creation/destruction of atoms in reactions.
Thomson's Plum Pudding Model (1897)
After discovering the electron, Thomson proposed the atom as a uniformly distributed positive charge "pudding" with electrons embedded as "plums".
▲ Thomson Model — electrons embedded in positive sphere
▼ Rutherford's Gold Foil Experiment disproved this — most alpha particles passed straight through
Rutherford's Nuclear Model (1911)
The gold foil experiment showed that most of an atom is empty space, with a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus at the center. Electrons orbit the nucleus like planets around the sun.
Failure: Classical electrodynamics predicts that orbiting electrons must radiate energy continuously and spiral into the nucleus in ~10⁻¹¹ s. The model cannot explain atomic spectra (discrete lines).
▲ Rutherford Nuclear Model
▼ Led to Bohr's quantized orbit model
§2 — Bohr's Model of the Hydrogen Atom (1913)
  • Postulate 1 — Stationary States: Electrons revolve around the nucleus in certain fixed circular orbits (stationary states) without radiating energy. Each orbit has a fixed energy level.
  • Postulate 2 — Quantization of Angular Momentum: Only those orbits are permitted in which the angular momentum of the electron is an integral multiple of ℏ = h/2π.
$mvr = n\hbar = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}$
where n = 1, 2, 3, … (principal quantum number), m = electron mass, v = velocity, r = orbit radius
  • Postulate 3 — Quantum Jumps & Photon Emission: An electron can jump from a higher energy orbit (E₂) to a lower orbit (E₁) by emitting a photon of frequency ν such that:
$h\nu = E_2 - E_1$
Energy of emitted/absorbed photon equals the energy difference between orbits
Bohr Radius and Energy
$r_n = n^2 a_0 = n^2 \times 0.529\,\text{Å}$
Radius of nth orbit (a₀ = Bohr radius = 0.529 × 10⁻¹⁰ m)
$E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2}\,\text{eV}$
Energy of nth orbit for hydrogen (negative = bound state)
Spectral Series of Hydrogen
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R_\infty\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$
Rydberg formula. R∞ = 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹ (Rydberg constant)
Seriesn₁n₂Region
Lyman12,3,4…Ultraviolet
Balmer23,4,5…Visible
Paschen34,5,6…Infrared
Brackett45,6,7…Infrared
Pfund56,7,8…Far IR
Humphreys67,8,9…Far IR
▲ Bohr Orbits and Spectral Transitions
▼ Each arrow = electron transition emitting a photon of specific wavelength
Limitations of Bohr's Model: Fails for multi-electron atoms. Cannot explain Zeeman effect (splitting in magnetic field) or Stark effect (splitting in electric field). No explanation for the intensity or fine structure of spectral lines. Violates Heisenberg's uncertainty principle.
§3 — De Broglie's Wave-Particle Duality (1924)
Louis de Broglie proposed that just as light (waves) can behave as particles (photons), material particles can also exhibit wave behavior. Every moving particle has an associated matter wave.
$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{mv}$
De Broglie wavelength. h = Planck's constant (6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s), p = momentum, m = mass, v = velocity
  • For an electron in Bohr orbit: The de Broglie wavelength of the electron must fit exactly as a standing wave around the orbit circumference, giving: 2πr = nλ → nλ = 2πr → mvr = nh/2π. This beautifully justifies Bohr's angular momentum quantization!
▲ Standing wave condition on Bohr orbit (n=4 shown)
▼ Matter wave (blue) must close on itself exactly — only integer wavelengths allowed
Electron at 1 eV
λ ≈ 1.23 nm
Electron at 100 eV
λ ≈ 0.123 nm
Baseball (0.14 kg, 40 m/s)
λ ≈ 10⁻³⁴ m
Proton at 1 MeV
λ ≈ 2.86 × 10⁻¹⁴ m
  • Key insight: Wave nature is significant only for very light particles (electrons, protons). Macroscopic objects have wavelengths far too small to observe — hence we don't see classical objects diffracting.
§4 — Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle (1927)
Werner Heisenberg proved that it is fundamentally impossible to simultaneously measure both the exact position and exact momentum of a quantum particle. This is not a measurement limitation — it is an intrinsic property of nature.
$\Delta x \cdot \Delta p_x \geq \dfrac{\hbar}{2}$
Position-momentum uncertainty. Δx = uncertainty in position, Δp = uncertainty in momentum, ℏ = h/2π
$\Delta E \cdot \Delta t \geq \dfrac{\hbar}{2}$
Energy-time uncertainty. ΔE = uncertainty in energy, Δt = uncertainty in time
  • Physical interpretation: If we try to pinpoint an electron's exact location (small Δx), its momentum becomes completely unknown (large Δp), and vice versa. Electrons cannot be said to have definite trajectories — only probability distributions.
  • Why Bohr's circular orbits are impossible: If an electron were on a definite circular orbit, both its position AND momentum would be known simultaneously — violating the uncertainty principle. This is why we use orbitals (probability clouds) instead of orbits.
Consequence: An electron confined to the nucleus (Δx ≈ 10⁻¹⁵ m) would need momentum Δp ≥ ℏ/(2 × 10⁻¹⁵) ≈ 33 MeV/c, implying kinetic energy far exceeding nuclear binding energy. Electrons cannot exist inside the nucleus — proving the nuclear model's consistency.
§5 — Schrödinger's Wave Equation
Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE)
Governs the evolution of a quantum state over time. It is the quantum equivalent of Newton's second law.
$i\hbar\,\dfrac{\partial\Psi(\mathbf{r},t)}{\partial t} = \hat{H}\,\Psi(\mathbf{r},t)$
Ĥ = Hamiltonian operator = T̂ + V̂ (kinetic + potential energy). Ψ = wavefunction. i = √−1
$\hat{H} = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2 + V(\mathbf{r},t)$
∇² = Laplacian operator = ∂²/∂x² + ∂²/∂y² + ∂²/∂z²
Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation (TISE)
For stationary states (constant potential), the time dependence separates out: Ψ(r,t) = ψ(r)·e^(−iEt/ℏ). The spatial part ψ(r) satisfies:
$\hat{H}\,\psi(\mathbf{r}) = E\,\psi(\mathbf{r})$
Eigenvalue equation — E = energy eigenvalue, ψ = spatial wavefunction
$-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\nabla^2\psi + V(r)\psi = E\psi$
Expanded form. For hydrogen: V(r) = −e²/(4πε₀r)
In Spherical Coordinates
For the hydrogen atom the potential is spherically symmetric V = V(r), so we use spherical coordinates (r, θ, φ). The equation separates into radial and angular parts via ψ(r,θ,φ) = R(r) · Y(θ,φ):
$\psi_{nlm}(r,\theta,\varphi) = R_{nl}(r)\cdot Y_l^m(\theta,\varphi)$
Complete hydrogen wavefunction = Radial part × Angular part (spherical harmonic)
Radial Wavefunction Rₙₗ(r)
$R_{nl}(r) = N_{nl}\,e^{-\rho/2}\,\rho^l\,L_{n-l-1}^{(2l+1)}(\rho)$
ρ = 2r/(na₀). Nₙₗ = normalization constant. Lₙ₋ₗ₋₁^(2l+1) = associated Laguerre polynomial
$N_{nl} = -\!\left[\left(\dfrac{2}{na_0}\right)^{\!3}\dfrac{(n-l-1)!}{2n\,(n+l)!}\right]^{\!1/2}$
Normalization constant for radial wavefunction
nl$R_{nl}(r)$
10 (1s)$2\left(\dfrac{1}{a_0}\right)^{3/2} e^{-r/a_0}$
20 (2s)$\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\!\left(2-\dfrac{r}{a_0}\right)e^{-r/2a_0}$
21 (2p)$\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}\left(\dfrac{1}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\dfrac{r}{a_0}\,e^{-r/2a_0}$
30 (3s)$\dfrac{2}{81\sqrt{3}}\left(\dfrac{1}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\!\left(27-\dfrac{18r}{a_0}+\dfrac{2r^2}{a_0^2}\right)e^{-r/3a_0}$
31 (3p)$\dfrac{8}{27\sqrt{6}}\left(\dfrac{1}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\!\left(6-\dfrac{r}{a_0}\right)\dfrac{r}{a_0}\,e^{-r/3a_0}$
32 (3d)$\dfrac{4}{81\sqrt{30}}\left(\dfrac{1}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{a_0}\right)^{2}e^{-r/3a_0}$
Angular Wavefunction Yₗₘ(θ,φ) — Spherical Harmonics
$Y_l^m(\theta,\varphi) = \Theta_{lm}(\theta)\cdot\Phi_m(\varphi)$
Separable into polar (θ) and azimuthal (φ) parts
$\Phi_m(\varphi) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{im\varphi}$
Azimuthal part. m = magnetic quantum number = 0, ±1, ±2, …, ±l
$\Theta_{lm}(\theta) = N_{lm}\,P_l^{|m|}(\cos\theta)$
Polar part. Pₗ|ᵐ| = associated Legendre polynomial
▲ Real spherical harmonics |Yₗₘ(θ,φ)|² — angular probability shapes
▼ Angular probability distributions: s = sphere, p = dumbbells, d = 4-lobes, f = complex multi-lobe
Born's Probability Interpretation
$P(\mathbf{r})\,dV = |\psi(\mathbf{r})|^2\,dV$
Probability of finding the electron in volume element dV at position r
$\displaystyle\int|\psi|^2\,dV = 1$
Normalization condition — electron must be found somewhere
Radial Probability Distribution
$P(r) = r^2\,|R_{nl}(r)|^2$
Probability of finding electron at radius r (integrated over all angles). The r² factor comes from the spherical volume element r² sin θ dr dθ dφ
§6 — Quantum Numbers
  • Principal Quantum Number (n): Determines the energy level and size of the orbital. n = 1, 2, 3, 4, … (positive integers). Higher n → larger orbital, higher energy, electron farther from nucleus.
$E_n = -\dfrac{13.6}{n^2}\,\text{eV}$
  • Azimuthal / Angular Momentum Quantum Number (l): Determines the shape of the orbital. l = 0, 1, 2, …, (n−1). Also gives the angular momentum magnitude: L = ℏ√(l(l+1)).
lSubshellShapeNo. of orbitals
0sSphere1
1pDumbbell3
2dDouble dumbbell / clover5
3fComplex multi-lobe7
4gVery complex9
  • Magnetic Quantum Number (mₗ): Determines the spatial orientation of the orbital. mₗ = −l, −l+1, …, 0, …, l−1, l. Total (2l+1) values. Gives z-component of angular momentum: Lz = mₗℏ.
  • Spin Quantum Number (mₛ): Intrinsic angular momentum of the electron. mₛ = +½ (spin-up, ↑) or −½ (spin-down, ↓). Not derived from Schrödinger equation — requires relativistic Dirac equation.
$S = \hbar\sqrt{s(s+1)} = \dfrac{\hbar\sqrt{3}}{2}\quad(s=\tfrac{1}{2})$
Total spin angular momentum magnitude
n (principal)
1, 2, 3, 4 …
l (azimuthal)
0 → n−1
mₗ (magnetic)
−l → +l
mₛ (spin)
+½ or −½
  • Total orbitals per shell: n² orbitals per shell n (ignoring spin). With spin: 2n² electrons maximum per shell.
§7 — Nodes and Nodal Surfaces
A node is a surface where the wavefunction ψ = 0 (and thus the probability density |ψ|² = 0 — the electron cannot be found there).
  • Radial nodes (spherical surfaces): Where Rₙₗ(r) = 0. Number = n − l − 1.
  • Angular nodes (planes/cones): Where Yₗₘ = 0. Number = l.
  • Total nodes = n − 1 = radial + angular nodes.
OrbitalnlRadial nodesAngular nodesTotal
1s10000
2s20101
2p21011
3s30202
3p31112
3d32022
4f43033
▲ Radial probability distribution P(r) = r²|Rₙₗ|² showing radial nodes
▼ 1s: no nodes (smooth peak). 2s: one radial node (crosses zero, then second peak)
§8 — Electronic Configuration Rules
Aufbau Principle
  • Aufbau Principle: Electrons fill orbitals starting from the lowest energy level upward. Orbital energy order (approx): 1s < 2s < 2p < 3s < 3p < 4s < 3d < 4p < 5s < 4d < 5p < 6s < 4f < 5d …
▲ Aufbau Diagonal Rule — fill in diagonal direction
▼ (n+l) rule: fill in order of increasing (n+l); if equal (n+l), fill lower n first
Pauli Exclusion Principle (1925)
  • Pauli Exclusion Principle: No two electrons in an atom can have the same set of all four quantum numbers (n, l, mₗ, mₛ).
  • Max electrons per orbital: Each orbital can hold at most 2 electrons.
  • Spin requirement: The two electrons in an orbital must have opposite spins (↑↓).
Hund's Rule of Maximum Multiplicity
  • Hund's Rule: When electrons occupy degenerate orbitals (same energy), they fill singly first before pairing up.
  • Parallel spins first: Electrons fill with parallel spins (all ↑) across degenerate orbitals (e.g. three 2p orbitals).
  • Why? Minimizes electron-electron repulsion and maximizes exchange energy, lowering total energy.
▲ Hund's Rule — filling 2p orbitals (Carbon: [He] 2s² 2p²)
▼ Nitrogen 2p³: all three p orbitals singly occupied with parallel spins — maximum stability
§9 — Orbital Shapes & Orientations
▲ s, p, d orbital cross-sections (probability density isosurfaces)
▼ Phase sign (+/−) matters for bonding: overlapping same-sign lobes = bonding MO; opposite = antibonding
Subshelllmₗ valuesNo. orbitalsMax electrons
s0012
p1−1, 0, +136
d2−2,−1,0,+1,+2510
f3−3…+3714
g4−4…+4918
h5−5…+51122
§10 — Advanced Rules & Effects
Shielding & Effective Nuclear Charge
  • Shielding (Screening): Inner electrons repel outer electrons, reducing the effective nuclear charge experienced by an outer electron. Zeff = Z − σ, where σ = shielding constant.
$Z_{\text{eff}} = Z - \sigma$
Slater's rules give σ based on which orbitals shield each other
  • Penetration: s orbitals penetrate closer to nucleus than p, which penetrate more than d. More penetrating orbitals experience less shielding → lower energy. Hence: s < p < d < f in energy within same n.
Exchange Energy
  • Exchange Energy: Electrons with parallel spins in different orbitals of the same subshell can exchange positions. Each exchange lowers the energy. More parallel spins = more exchanges = lower energy. This explains the extra stability of half-filled (d⁵, f⁷) and fully filled (d¹⁰, f¹⁴) subshells.
Anomalous Electronic Configurations
  • Chromium (Cr, Z=24): Expected [Ar]3d⁴4s². Actual: [Ar]3d⁵4s¹ — half-filled d is extra stable.
  • Copper (Cu, Z=29): Expected [Ar]3d⁹4s². Actual: [Ar]3d¹⁰4s¹ — fully filled d is extra stable.
Zeeman Effect
  • Zeeman Effect: In an external magnetic field B, each energy level with angular momentum l splits into (2l+1) sub-levels. The energy shift is: ΔE = mₗ μ_B B, where μ_B = eℏ/2mₑ = Bohr magneton. Normal Zeeman: 3 lines. Anomalous Zeeman: more lines (requires electron spin).
$\Delta E = m_l\,\mu_B\,B$
Energy splitting per sub-level in external field B. μ_B = 9.274 × 10⁻²⁴ J/T
Stark Effect
  • Stark Effect: Splitting of spectral lines in an external electric field. The electric field mixes hydrogen states of different l (but same n), removing their degeneracy. Linear Stark effect for hydrogen; quadratic for others.
Spin-Orbit Coupling
  • Spin-Orbit Coupling: The magnetic moment from the electron's orbital motion interacts with its spin magnetic moment. This couples L and S into a total angular momentum J = L + S. Gives fine structure of spectral lines. Notation: ²ˢ⁺¹Lⱼ (term symbol).
$E_{so} \propto \mathbf{L}\cdot\mathbf{S} = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[J(J+1)-L(L+1)-S(S+1)\bigr]\hbar^2$
Spin-orbit energy. J ranges from |L−S| to L+S
Selection Rules for Spectral Transitions
  • Electric dipole transitions (dominant) must satisfy: Δl = ±1, Δmₗ = 0, ±1, Δmₛ = 0, Δn = any integer. Transitions with Δl = 0 or Δl = ±2, etc. are forbidden (weak or absent).
Relativistic Effects (Heavy Atoms)
  • Relativistic contraction: For heavy atoms (Z > 70), inner electrons travel at significant fractions of c. Their effective mass increases → s and p orbitals contract (become more stable), d and f orbitals expand (relatively). This is why gold is yellow (6s orbital lowered) and mercury is liquid at room temperature.
Photoelectric Effect (Einstein, 1905)
  • Photoelectric Effect: When light of frequency ν falls on a metal, electrons are ejected if hν ≥ φ (work function). This proved light is quantized (photons). KE_max = hν − φ.
$KE_{\max} = h\nu - \varphi = h(\nu - \nu_0)$
h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, φ = work function, ν₀ = threshold frequency
Compton Scattering
  • Compton Effect (1923): X-rays scattered by electrons shift to longer wavelengths. Proved photons have momentum p = h/λ. Compton shift: Δλ = (h/mₑc)(1 − cosθ).
$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_c(1-\cos\theta),\quad \lambda_c = \dfrac{h}{m_e c} = 2.426\,\text{pm}$
λ_c = Compton wavelength of electron
Pauli's Exclusion & the Periodic Table
  • Shell capacity 2n² directly explains periodic table structure: Period 1 = 2 elements (1s), Period 2 = 8 (2s,2p), Period 3 = 8 (3s,3p), Period 4 = 18 (4s,3d,4p), Period 5 = 18, Period 6 = 32 (includes 4f lanthanides), Period 7 = 32 (includes 5f actinides).
§11 — Multi-Electron Atoms & Approximations
The Schrödinger equation cannot be solved exactly for atoms with more than one electron due to the electron-electron repulsion term. Several approximation methods are used:
Hartree-Fock Self-Consistent Field (SCF)
  • Hartree-Fock: Each electron is treated as moving in the average field of all other electrons. The Fock operator F̂ψᵢ = εᵢψᵢ is solved iteratively until self-consistent. The wavefunction is a Slater determinant (antisymmetric product) automatically satisfying Pauli's principle.
Density Functional Theory (DFT)
  • DFT (Hohenberg-Kohn, 1964): All ground-state properties are determined by the electron density ρ(r) alone. The energy is a functional E[ρ]. Modern quantum chemistry and material science rely heavily on DFT for efficiency.
Perturbation Theory
  • First-order perturbation: E₁ = E₀ + ⟨ψ₀|Ĥ′|ψ₀⟩. Used for calculating fine structure, Zeeman effect, Stark effect corrections to hydrogen energy levels.

⛛ Molecular Orbital Theory — Complete Reference

§1 — Core Postulates of MOT
  • LCAO Principle: Molecular orbitals are formed by Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals. N atomic orbitals combine to give exactly N molecular orbitals.
  • Bonding MO (ψ+): Constructive interference (same phase). Electron density concentrated between nuclei → lower energy → bond formed.
  • Antibonding MO (ψ*): Destructive interference (opposite phase). Nodal plane between nuclei → higher energy → nuclei repel.
  • Non-bonding MO: No symmetry-matched partner → same energy as original AO. Does not contribute to bonding.
  • Energy splitting: Bonding MO lowered by same amount antibonding MO is raised relative to AO energy.
  • Aufbau in MOs: Fill from lowest energy upward, obeying Pauli Exclusion and Hund’s Rule.
  • Pauli Exclusion: Each MO holds at most 2 electrons with opposite spins.
  • Hund’s Rule: Degenerate MOs (π2p, π*2p pairs) fill singly first with parallel spins.
§2 — Bond Order
$\text{Bond Order} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bonding}} - N_{\text{antibonding}}}{2}$
N = electrons in bonding/antibonding MOs
  • Bond Order > 0: Stable molecule. Higher bond order → stronger, shorter bond.
  • Bond Order = 0: Molecule does not exist (e.g. He₂, bond order = 0).
  • Fractional bond orders are possible: NO has bond order 2.5, O₂⁺ has 2.5.
  • Correlation: Higher bond order → shorter bond length → higher bond dissociation energy.
swipe to scroll →
Molecule Total e⁻ Bond e⁻ Anti e⁻ B.O. Unpaired Property
H₂22010Stable, diamagnetic
He₂42200Does not exist
Li₂64210Stable, diamagnetic
Be₂84400Does not exist
B₂106412Paramagnetic
C₂128420Stable, diamagnetic
N₂1410430Very stable, triple bond
O₂1610622Paramagnetic
F₂1810810Stable, diamagnetic
Ne₂20101000Does not exist
NO151052.51Paramagnetic, stable
B.O. = Bond Order · Anti = Antibonding e⁻
§3 — MO Energy Diagrams & O₂ Paramagnetism
  • Li₂–N₂ order: σ1s < σ*1s < σ2s < σ*2s < π2p = π2p < σ2p < π*2p = π*2p < σ*2p
  • O₂–Ne₂ order: σ2p drops below π2p due to s-p mixing: σ2s < σ*2s < σ2p < π2p = π2p < π*2p = π*2p < σ*2p
  • O₂ paramagnetism: Two degenerate π*2p MOs each get 1 electron (Hund’s Rule) → 2 unpaired e⁻ → paramagnetic. MOT predicts this; VBT fails.
  • Benzene delocalization: MOs are delocalized over the entire ring → all C–C bonds equal length (1.40 Å) — explained by MOT, not VBT resonance.
▲ O₂ MO Energy Diagram (bond order 2, paramagnetic)
▼ Cyan = bonding MO, Red/Orange = antibonding. Arrows = electron spins (↑↓ paired, ↑ unpaired)
§4 — Bond Types by |mₗ|
  • σ (sigma, |m|=0): Head-on overlap along bond axis. Cylindrically symmetric. Strongest. All s-s, pz-pz overlaps.
  • π (pi, |m|=1): Side-on overlap. Two lobes above/below axis. p-p lateral or d-p. Weaker than σ.
  • δ (delta, |m|=2): Face-to-face d orbital overlap. Four lobes. Metal-metal multiple bonds.
  • φ (phi, |m|=3): f orbital face overlap. Six lobes. Actinide compounds.
  • γ,η,θ,ι,κ… (|m|=4,5,6,7,8…): Higher-order bonds from higher angular momentum orbitals. Theoretical; visualizable in this app.
  • General rule: Bond type = |mₗ| of orbital symmetry around bond axis. Must match for both atoms.
Bond|m|LobesOrbitals
σ02s, pz, dz², fz³
π14px, py, dxz, dyz
δ28dxy, dx²-y²
φ312f orbitals
γ416g orbitals
η520h orbitals
θ,ι,κ…6,7,8…24,28,32…i, j, k…
§5 — LCAO Wavefunction & Phase
$\psi_{\pm} = \psi_A \pm \psi_B$
ψ+ = bonding MO (constructive), ψ− = antibonding MO (destructive)
$\psi_{MO} = \psi_A(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{R}_A) + p_b\,\psi_B(\mathbf{r}-\mathbf{R}_B)$
pₛ = +1 bonding, −1 antibonding. R_A, R_B = nuclear positions
  • Positive phase (cyan): ψ > 0. Constructive overlap of same-sign lobes → bonding region.
  • Negative phase (red): ψ < 0. Destructive overlap → nodal plane between nuclei.
  • Probability density |ψ|² is always positive — electron cloud has no negative density.
  • Phase sign only matters for relative phase between the two AOs, not absolute sign.
§6 — Overlap Integral & Selection Rules
$S_{AB} = \int \psi_A^*\,\psi_B\,dV$
Overlap integral. S = 0 → no bonding possible by symmetry
  • Symmetry rule: AOs must have same symmetry (same |mₗ|) around bond axis to form MOs.
  • Energy matching: AOs of similar energy form stronger MOs. Large ΔE → weak interaction.
  • Heteronuclear MOs: More electronegative atom contributes more to bonding MO; less electronegative to antibonding MO.
§7 — Magnetic Properties
$\mu = \sqrt{n(n+2)}\,\mu_B$
Spin-only magnetic moment. n = unpaired electrons, μ_B = 9.274 × 10⁻²⁴ J/T
  • Diamagnetic: All electrons paired → no net moment → repelled by B field. (N₂, H₂, F₂)
  • Paramagnetic: Unpaired electrons → net moment → attracted by B field. (O₂: n=2, NO: n=1)
  • MOT triumph: Correctly predicts O₂ paramagnetism. VBT (Lewis structure) incorrectly shows O₂ as diamagnetic.
§8 — Internuclear Distance Control
INTERNUCLEAR DISTANCE 2.0 a₀
0.8 a₀← drag →500 a₀
  • Equilibrium distance: Where bonding stabilization equals nuclear repulsion. Shorter for higher bond order.
  • d → ∞: MO degenerates to original AOs. Clouds separate completely.
  • d → 0: Extreme nuclear repulsion dominates. United-atom limit.
  • Examples: H₂ = 0.74 Å, N₂ = 1.10 Å, O₂ = 1.21 Å, F₂ = 1.42 Å

▲ COMPOUND BUILDER ▲
⟳

⬡ Compound Builder — Chemical Bonding Theory

§1 — Chemical Bonds
▲ Bond types — electron behaviour
  • Ionic bond: Transfer of electrons from metal to non-metal. Forms cation (+) and anion (−). Strong electrostatic attraction. High melting points. Examples: NaCl, MgO, CaCl₂.
  • Covalent bond: Sharing of electrons between non-metals. Can be single (σ), double (σ+π), or triple (σ+2π). Examples: H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.
  • Metallic bond: Delocalized electrons in a "sea" around metal cation lattice. Explains conductivity, malleability, and lustre.
  • Coordinate/dative bond: Both electrons in the bond come from one atom (the donor). Example: NH₃ → BF₃, H₃O⁺.
§2 — Bond Properties
▲ Single, double, triple bonds — length vs strength
PropertySingleDoubleTriple
Bond order123
Bond lengthLongestMediumShortest
Bond energyWeakestMediumStrongest
RotationFree (σ)RestrictedRestricted
σ bonds111
π bonds012
  • Bond length trend: As bond order increases, atoms are pulled closer → shorter, stronger bonds. C–C: 1.54 Å → C=C: 1.34 Å → C≡C: 1.20 Å.
  • Bond dissociation energy: Energy to break 1 mol of bonds in gas phase. Higher bond order → more energy needed.
  • Electronegativity difference: ΔEN > 1.7 → ionic · 0.4–1.7 → polar covalent · < 0.4 → nonpolar covalent.
§3 — Lewis Structures & Formal Charge
▲ Lewis structures — H₂O, CO₂, NH₃
  • Lewis structure: Shows all valence electrons as dots; shared pairs are bonds (lines), unshared are lone pairs.
  • Octet rule: Main-group elements fill to 8 valence electrons. H needs only 2 (duet).
  • Formal charge: FC = Valence e⁻ − Lone pair e⁻ − ½(Bonding e⁻). Best structure minimises FC and puts negative FC on more electronegative atom.
  • Resonance: When multiple valid Lewis structures exist, the real molecule is the hybrid of all — e.g. benzene, NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻.
  • Exceptions to octet: Electron deficient (BF₃, 6e⁻), odd-electron radicals (NO·), expanded octets (PCl₅, SF₆).
§4 — Intermolecular Forces
▲ Relative strength of intermolecular forces
  • London dispersion: Temporary dipoles from electron movement. Present in ALL molecules. Strength increases with molecular size and surface area.
  • Dipole-dipole: Between permanent dipoles of polar molecules. Stronger than dispersion for similar-sized molecules.
  • Hydrogen bonding: H bonded to N, O, or F interacts with lone pair on another N, O, or F. Explains H₂O boiling at 100°C vs H₂S at −60°C.
  • Ion-dipole: Between an ion and polar molecule. Strongest IMF. Drives dissolution of ionic solids in water.
§5 — Molecular Geometry (VSEPR)
▲ Common molecular shapes
DomainsLone pairsGeometryAngle
20Linear180°
30Trigonal planar120°
31Bent~119°
40Tetrahedral109.5°
41Trigonal pyramidal~107°
42Bent~104.5°
50Trig. bipyramidal90°/120°
60Octahedral90°
§6 — Polarity & Dipole Moments
▲ Polar vs nonpolar — dipole cancellation
  • Bond polarity: Arises when atoms in a bond have different electronegativities. More electronegative atom carries δ−.
  • Molecular dipole: Vector sum of all bond dipoles. Symmetric geometry → dipoles cancel → nonpolar (CO₂, BF₃, CCl₄).
  • Polar molecules: Net dipole ≠ 0. Examples: H₂O (1.85 D), NH₃ (1.47 D), HF (1.91 D). Like dissolves like.
  • Dipole moment: $\mu = q \times d$ in Debye units (D). Determines solubility, boiling point, and intermolecular interactions.
§7 — Wedge-Dash Notation
▲ 3D representation of CH₄ using wedge-dash
  • Solid line (—): Bond lies in the plane of the paper.
  • Solid wedge (▶): Bond points toward you — out of the page. Drawn as a filled triangle.
  • Dashed wedge (▷): Bond points away from you — into the page. Drawn as dashed lines.
  • Chirality: A carbon bonded to 4 different groups is a stereocentre. The two mirror-image forms (enantiomers) are non-superimposable and can have opposite biological effects.

✦ Hybridization Theory — Complete Reference

§1 — Core Postulates
§2 — Quick Reference Table
TypeOrbitalsGeometryAngleExample
sp2Linear180°BeCl₂, C₂H₂
sp²3Trigonal planar120°BF₃, C₂H₄
sp³4Tetrahedral109.5°CH₄, NH₃
sp³d5Trig. bipyramidal90°/120°PCl₅
sp³d²6Octahedral90°SF₆
dsp²4Square planar90°[PtCl₄]²⁻
d²sp³6Octahedral (inner)90°[Fe(CN)₆]³⁻
sp³d³7Pent. bipyramidal72°/90°IF₇
§3 — Each Hybridization with Orbital Diagrams
§4 — VSEPR & Lone Pair Effects
  • VSEPR principle: Electron pairs repel each other and arrange to be as far apart as possible — determines molecular shape.
  • Lone pair > bonding pair repulsion: Lone pairs occupy more angular space, compressing bond angles between bonding pairs.
  • CH₄ vs NH₃ vs H₂O: All sp³ — but angles decrease: 109.5° → 107° → 104.5° as lone pairs increase from 0 → 1 → 2.
  • Multiple bonds: A double or triple bond counts as ONE electron domain for VSEPR — same as a single bond for geometry purposes.
  • Axial vs equatorial (sp³d): Lone pairs prefer equatorial positions (120° apart) over axial (90° apart) to minimize repulsion.
§5 — σ and π Bonds
  • σ bond: Head-on overlap. First bond formed. Cylindrically symmetric around bond axis. Allows free rotation.
  • π bond: Side-on overlap of unhybridized p orbitals. Second or third bond only. Has a nodal plane along axis. Restricts rotation → cis/trans isomers possible.
  • Single bond = 1σ · Double bond = 1σ + 1π · Triple bond = 1σ + 2π
  • σ bonds are stronger than π bonds due to greater orbital overlap along the internuclear axis.
  • Delocalized π: In benzene, 6 unhybridized p orbitals overlap continuously around the ring, forming a delocalized π cloud — explains aromatic stability and equal C–C bond lengths (1.40 Å).
